texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: ABSTRACT
texts.text: ABSTRACT

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent observations indicate that stripped-envelope core-collapse supernovae are often surrounded by dense circumstellar material (CSM). Motivated by this, we develop an analytic model to systematically study the dynamics of long gamma-ray burst (LGRB) jet propagation in various CSM environments. We derive a general expression for the jet head velocity ( β h ) and breakout time ( t b ) valid across Newtonian, relativistic, and intermediate regimes, accounting for a previously unrecognized dependence on 1 -β h . Our results highlight a fundamental distinction between jet propagation in massive stars, where β h ≪ 1, and in extended CSM, where 1 -β h ≪ 1. We establish an analytic success/failure criterion for jets and express it in terms of jet and CSM parameters, revealing a strong dependence on CSM radius. To quantify the relativistic nature of the jet-cocoon system, we introduce the energy-weighted proper velocity Γ β . We identify three possible jet outcomes-(a) successful jets (Γ β ∼ 10 -100), (b) barely failed jets (Γ β ∼ 1), and (c) completely failed jets (Γ β ∼ 0 . 1)-and constrain their respective jet/CSM parameter spaces. We show that in (b) and (c), large CSM radii can result in luminous fast blue optical transients via cocoon cooling emission. This theoretical framework provides a basis for future observational and theoretical studies to understand the link between LGRBs, intermediate GRBs, low-luminosity LGRBs, and their environments.

We establish an analytic success/failure criterion for jets and express it in terms of jet and CSM parameters, revealing a strong dependence on CSM radius. To quantify the relativistic nature of the jet-cocoon system, we introduce the energy-weighted proper velocity Γ β . We identify three possible jet outcomes-(a) successful jets (Γ β ∼ 10 -100), (b) barely failed jets (Γ β ∼ 1), and (c) completely failed jets (Γ β ∼ 0 . 1)-and constrain their respective jet/CSM parameter spaces. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: 1. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) are observed in the form of prompt emission ( ∼ 1 -100 s), making them the most energetic electromagnetic transients in the Universe (Klebesadel et al. 1973). GRBs are understood to originate from highly relativistic jets (Rhoads 1997; Piran 2004). In the collapsar model, long-duration GRBs (LGRBs) are explained by the core-collapse of massive stars, forming an accretion disk around a central compact object, which subsequently powers relativistic jets (Woosley 1993; MacFadyen & Woosley 1999). Consequently, LGRBs are expected to be associated with the deaths of massive stars.

texts.orig: Follow-up observations of LGRBs have confirmed their connection to broad-lined (BL) Type Ic supernovae (SNe), such as SN 1998bw (e.g., Galama et al. 1998; Iwamoto et al. 1998; Kulkarni et al. 1998) and SN 2003dh (e.g., Stanek et al. 2003), etc., providing strong observational support for the collapsar model. These GRB-SN associations suggest that the progenitors are stripped-envelope stars, i.e., Wolf-Rayet (WR) stars. This is consistent with the collapsar scenario, as such stripped stars are compact, making them easier to penetrate by relativistic jets launched at their cores, given the expected short accretion timescales and observed emission timescales ( ∼ 10 2 s; Matzner 2003; Bromberg et al. 2011b,a; Suwa & Ioka 2011). Numerical simulations have confirmed that jet propagation through compact progenitors is feasible under these conditions (e.g., Aloy et al. 2000; Zhang et al. 2004; Lazzati et al. 2012; Mizuta & Ioka 2013; Hamidani et al. 2017; Harri- son et al. 2018; Piran et al. 2019; Gottlieb et al. 2021; Eisenberg et al. 2022; Pais et al. 2023).

texts.orig: Observations have also revealed a population of lowluminosity GRBs (LLGRBs), which are significantly fainter than their high-energy counterparts (e.g., GRB 060218/SN 2006aj; GRB 100316D/SN 2010bh; Campana et al. 2006; Starling et al. 2011), but are significantly more common than LGRBs due to their closer proximity (Soderberg et al. 2006c; Liang et al. 2007). Unlike cosmological LGRBs, LLGRBs have been proposed to arise from jets that fail to break out because they are choked within dense stellar or circumstellar environments (Nakar 2015), leading to much weaker prompt emissions possibly due to shock breakout (e.g., Campana et al. 2006; Waxman et al. 2007; Nakar & Sari 2012).
texts.text: Observations have also revealed a population of lowluminosity GRBs (LLGRBs), which are significantly fainter than their high-energy counterparts (e.g., GRB 060218/SN 2006aj; GRB 100316D/SN 2010bh; Campana et al. 2006; Starling et al. 2011), but are significantly more common than LGRBs due to their closer proximity (Soderberg et al. 2006c; Liang et al. 2007). Unlike cosmological LGRBs, LLGRBs have been proposed to arise from jets that fail to break out because they are choked within dense stellar or circumstellar environments (Nakar 2015), leading to much weaker prompt emissions possibly due to shock breakout (e.g., Campana et al. 2006; Waxman et al. 2007; Nakar & Sari 2012).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consequently, the presence of an extended circumstellar medium (CSM) has been proposed as a key factor in explaining LLGRBs (Nakar 2015; Suzuki & Maeda 2022; Suzuki et al. 2024; or, alternatively to account for delayed emission in LGRBs Pe'er & Ryde 2024). Furthermore, the potential for bright cooling emission from their jet cocoons has been pointed out (Nakauchi et al. 2013; Nakar 2015; Suzuki & Maeda 2022). Therefore, it has been discussed that the interaction of the relativistic jet with the CSM could produce distinct observational signatures, including fast blue optical transients (FBOTs) (Margutti et al. 2019; Gottlieb et al. 2022; Suzuki et al. 2024). In fact, rapid follow-up observations of LGRBs / LLGRBs detected by the recent Einstein Probe (EP) mission (sensitive to softer events, in ∼ 0 . 5 -4 keV) show a clear association with FBOTs (EP240414a: Sun et al. 2024; Srivastav et al. 2025; van Dalen et al. 2025; Bright et al. 2024; and EP250108a: Eyles-Ferris et al. 2025; Rastinejad et al. 2025; Srinivasaragavan et al. 2025; Li et al. 2025).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Observations of SNe associated with strippedenvelope progenitors (Types Ib, Ic, Ibn, and Icn), in the pre-SN or post-SN phase, revealed the presence of CSM typically with masses ∼ 0 . 01 -1 M ⊙ extended over ∼ 10 14 -10 16 cm (see Pastorello et al. 2007; Foley et al. 2007; Pastorello et al. 2008; Immler et al. 2008; Benetti et al. 2006; Sollerman et al. 2020; Clark et al. 2020; Dong et al. 2024; Pellegrino et al. 2024; also see Maeda & Moriya 2022). This suggests that stripped massive stars (WR, similar stars to LGRB progenitors) undergo enhanced mass loss episodes in the last years before their explosion.

texts.orig: CSM has also been observed in Type II SNe (e.g., Soderberg et al. 2006a; Smith et al. 2008; Ofek et al. 2013; Smith et al. 2014; Fransson et al. 2014; Yaron et al. 2017; F¨ orster et al. 2018; Strotjohann et al. 2021; also, in the recent SN II 2023ixf Jacobson-Gal´ an et al. 2023; Bostroem et al. 2023; Smith et al. 2023), which are more common than type Ibc SNe (Li et al. 2011). This points to the ubiquity of CSM and enhanced mass-loss CCSNe at the end of the life of massive stars in general.

texts.orig: The mechanism of this intense mass loss is often attributed to enhanced energy generation through nuclear burning at the late stage of stellar evolution (e.g., Arnett & Meakin 2011; Smith & Arnett 2014; Quataert & Shiode 2012; Leung et al. 2021), or to binary interaction (Ouchi & Maeda 2017; Kuncarayakti et al. 2018).

texts.orig: The ubiquity of CSM around massive stars motivates the investigation of how a given CSM influences collapsar jet propagation and the resulting observational signatures. While numerical simulations could be used to explore this, the large CSM radii make such simulations numerically challenging. Therefore, developing an analytic framework is important.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, we develop a generalized model for jet propagation in extended CSM to systematically explore how different CSM configurations influence jet dynamics, breakout conditions, and the observational diversity of GRBs and associated transients. This paper is structured as follows. In Section 2, we describe the physical setup. In Section 3, we present our new analytic results. In Section 4, we present the criterion for jet success or failure. In Section 5, we discuss the intermediate case between successful and failed jets. In Section 6, we discuss the implications on LGRBs, intermediate GRBs, LLGRBs, and associated transients. In Section 7, we discuss our results. In Section 8, we present our conclusions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Setup
texts.text: 2.1. Setup

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 1 illustrates the setup and key concepts referred to throughout the paper. We consider the collapsar scenario, where the progenitor is a stripped compact ( ∼ 10 10 -10 11 cm) massive WR star (Woosley 1993; MacFadyen & Woosley 1999). The progenitor is considered to be surrounded by an extended CSM, which was supposedly produced prior to the collapsar in an intense mass-loss phase. Such CSMs have originally been discussed as a cause of jet failure and an explanation for low luminosity GRBs (Nakar 2015). Here, we are interested in the dynamics of jet propagation in different CSMs and their impact on the jet propagation and emission. Therefore, for convenience, we consider a conventional LGRB jet, while we vary the parameters of the CSM in terms of radius ( R CSM ) and mass ( M CSM ) (see Section 2.4).

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 1. Illustration of the model setup and basic concepts. A LGRB jet (white) is launched from the compact progenitor (light green). The progenitor is surrounded by an extended CSM (dark green) characterized by its total mass ( M CSM ) and its radius ( R CSM ). After punching through the compact progenitor, the jet propagates through the CSM, producing a jet head (yellow) and a cocoon (inner and outer, in purple and blue, respectively) which collimates the jet [see equations (22), (23), (24), and (25)]. After t eng ∼ 100 s, the engine is turned off, but the jet survives inside the CSM and continues to propagate as long as the jet's tail ( β t ∼ 1, in white) does not catch up to the jet head ( β h , in yellow). We define the jet's timescale which is the physical size of the jet at a given time t in units of its speed c [see equation (35)]. Once the jet's tail caches up to the jet head, the jet fails and t jet = 0 [defined by equations (37)]. Also shown is the cocoon lateral width r c and the jet head radius r h [see equations (18) and (14)].

texts.orig: We analytically solve the collapsar-jet propagation in a given CSM, by finding key quantities, in particular the jet head velocity β h . We use the analytical model in Hamidani & Ioka (2021), which was based on previous works (e.g., Matzner 2003; Bromberg et al. 2011b; Mizuta & Ioka 2013; Harrison et al. 2018; Salafia et al. 2020; Hamidani et al. 2020) with several improvements to self-consistently solve the jet propagation.

texts.orig: As a note, our analysis considers only simple hydrodynamic jets, whereas numerical simulations suggest that magnetohydrodynamic jets are a more natural result (Blandford & Znajek 1977). 3D simulations indicate that purely hydrodynamic LGRB jets propagating through dense media are susceptible to becoming unstable due to Rayleigh-Taylor instability (Matsumoto & Masada 2019), whereas weakly magnetized jets exhibit immunity to this instability (Gottlieb et al. 2020). Although we do not explicitly incorporate magnetic fields in our analysis, our results may still be applicable to hydrodynamically stable jets that are expected to arise from weak magnetization.
texts.text: As a note, our analysis considers only simple hydrodynamic jets, whereas numerical simulations suggest that magnetohydrodynamic jets are a more natural result (Blandford & Znajek 1977). 3D simulations indicate that purely hydrodynamic LGRB jets propagating through dense media are susceptible to becoming unstable due to Rayleigh-Taylor instability (Matsumoto & Masada 2019), whereas weakly magnetized jets exhibit immunity to this instability (Gottlieb et al. 2020). Although we do not explicitly incorporate magnetic fields in our analysis, our results may still be applicable to hydrodynamically stable jets that are expected to arise from weak magnetization.

texts.orig: In the limit where the medium is too dense (or too extended) the jet's tail can catch up to the jet head inside the medium (at t = t ∗ and r = r ∗ ). This leads to a situation where t jet = 0 before the breakout, representing the condition where jet 'fails' to break out while maintaining its high terminal Lorentz factor (also called chocked or buried jet; red line in Figure 4). In the situation where the jet head is fast enough to breakout before the engine is turned off and the tail of the jet catches up to it, t jet > 0, it is considered that the jet breaks out 'successfully' (blue line in Figure 4); this jet can later contribute to the GRB prompt emission (see Bromberg et al. 2012, 2013; Nakar 2015; Duffell & Ho 2020; Pais et al. 2023 for a similar argument) and afterglow emission due to its high Lorentz factor.

texts.orig: The above criterion shows that the jet head can breakout successfully despite the engine being turned off ( t b > t eng ), in particular when 1 -β h ≪ 1. This is because in such a case, the jet tail, which represents the propagation of the information that the engine has turned off, is causally disconnected with the jet head at t ⩽ t b (considering a speed of sound c s ∼ c ). Hence, in the frame of the jet head, the information that the engine has turned off does not reach the jet head at t b and the jet head propagation continues as if the engine is still on.

texts.orig: It is understood that the cocoon is composed of two distinct components (see Figure 1): the low massdensity high-enthalpy inner cocoon; and the high massdensity low-enthalpy outer cocoon (also, shocked jet and shocked medium, respectively; see: Bromberg et al. 2011b; Nakar & Piran 2017). Previous works (Nakar & Piran 2017; Gottlieb et al. 2021; and particularly Eisenberg et al. 2022; Pais et al. 2023 in more detail) show that the asymptotic energy distributions of the different components of the jet-cocoon system are comprehensible when expressed using dE d log Γ β . In particular, the outer cocoon, the inner cocoon and the successful jet component can each be identified as different (approximately flat) components (see Gottlieb et al. 2021; Eisenberg et al. 2022; Pais et al. 2023).

texts.orig: In figure 6, we schematically show the expected energy distribution as a function of the proper velocity Γ β , for different jets. Case (a) shows a successful jet ( t b ≪ t eng / (1 -β h )) which results in a strong jet that carries most of the engine energy. Case (c) shows a completely failed jet ( t b ≫ t eng / (1 -β h )), where both the jet and the inner cocoon ended up being mixed with the outer cocoon, transferring all their energy to it. Case (b) represents an intermediate scenario, where the jet fails ( t b ≳ t eng / (1 -β h )), but moderately relativistic inner cocoon is present. This could be the case in circumstances where the breakout takes place shortly after the jet tail catches up to the jet head, before the inner cocoon is mixed with the outer cocoon, or in cases where the jet fails in a low-density medium. We refer to this case as 'barely failed' (after Eisenberg et al. 2022 in the context of jet propagation inside massive stars). Possible candidates for this case are: GRB 200826 (Ahumada et al. 2021), EP240414a (Sun et al. 2024; Hamidani et al. 2025), etc.

texts.orig: We attempt to find in which parameter space the intermediate case (b) occurs. In Section 4 we defined the parameter space where (a) successful jets and (c) failed jets are expected to occur. Both (b) and (c) should fulfill t b ⩾ t eng / (1 -β h ), and E c ∼ E eng . As pointed out in Nakar & Piran (2017); Hamidani & Ioka (2023a); Suzuki et al. (2024), the interaction between the inner and outer cocoons is highly dependent on the degree of mixing, which is poorly understood even from numerical simulations. An alternative is to predict where (b) is favored to occur is to quantify the relativistic degree of the whole system. This is because of the expected strong presence of the mildly relativistic inner cocoon in (b), which helps to discriminate between (b) and (c) [(a) also] (see dotted lines in Figure 6).

We show the corresponding parameter space (between the thin and solid lines indicated by the arrow in Figure 7; for t jet = t eng / 10). In this case, and in particular when t jet < 2 s, the duration of the prompt emission ( T 90 ) can be also shorter than 2 s and such an event may be classified as a short GRB (Bromberg et al. 2011a, 2012, 2013; Piran et al. 2013). Assuming that the prompt emission occurs at r ⩾ R CSM through the same mechanism as LGRBs, other emission properties, except for the duration, e.g., the emission spectrum, will be consistent with those of LGRBs. One possible example is GRB 200826 (Ahumada et al. 2021).

texts.orig: 6.3. No GRB & Aspherical Explosion
texts.text: 6.3. No GRB & Aspherical Explosion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For a sufficiently dense CSM as M CSM ≳ 1 M ⊙ , the jet is completely failed and the system is expected to transit to the non-relativistic regime, with no possibility for a LGRB/LLGRB-like prompt emission (d). Still, we expect an aspherical explosion powered by the non- relativistic cocoon, with an explosion energy E c ∼ E eng and a typical velocity β c ∼ √ 2 E eng / ( M CSM c 2 ) ∼ 0 . 1 -0 . 01. We expect the degree of asphericity to decrease as the CSM radius increases (from left to right in Figure 7), with the explosion becoming near-spherical in the limit where R CSM (and M CSM ) is very large. This jet-driven explosion could be observed in the form of a BL-type SN (e.g., Nakauchi et al. 2015; Piran et al. 2019; Irwin et al. 2019; Eisenberg et al. 2022; Pais et al. 2023) or in the form of an aspherical explosion (see also Section 7.1).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the failed cases with an intermediate-mass CSM of a few × 10 -2 M ⊙ ≲ M CSM ≲ M ⊙ , the outer cocoon breaks out of the CSM with a trans-relativistic velocity, Γ β ≲ 1. The shock breakout produces soft to hard Xray photons, with a duration determined by the shock crossing timescale at the outer edge of the CSM (Nakar & Sari 2012). In particular, for R CSM ∼ 10 13 -14 cm, the emission properties are consistent with those of the observed LLGRBs, such as GRB 060218, which has L iso , peak ∼ 10 48 erg s -1 and T 90 ∼ 10 3 s (Nakar 2015; Irwin & Chevalier 2016; Irwin & Hotokezaka 2024). The lack of a strong relativistic component in this parameter space is expected to produce an afterglow emission that is much dimmer than that of LGRBs (as in GRB 060218; Campana et al. 2006; Soderberg et al. 2006b; Pian et al. 2006; Soderberg et al. 2006c).

texts.orig: If an intermediate-mass CSM with a few × 10 -2 M ⊙ ≲ M CSM ≲ M ⊙ has a more extended CSM with R CSM ≳ 10 14 cm and a conventional GRB jet is failed inside it, the associated quasi-thermal shock breakout emission becomes brighter and longer than the observed LLGRBs. Such a transient has not been confirmed so far. On the other hand, such extended CSM has been inferred from observations of interaction-powered stripped-envelope SNe, or type Ibn/Icn SNe (see Pastorello et al. 2007; Foley et al. 2007; Pastorello et al. 2008; Immler et al. 2008; Benetti et al. 2006; Sollerman et al. 2020; Clark et al. 2020; Dong et al. 2024; Pellegrino et al. 2024; also see Maeda & Moriya 2022). This implies that these observed events are from a distinctly different population, with no LGRB-like jets produced.

texts.orig: While non-relativistic shock breakout emission from the outer cocoon can occur simultaneously, it is expected to be subdominant compared to the prompt emission from the mildly relativistic jet (and its early afterglow). In the barely failed scenario, the mildly relativistic inner cocoon component breaks out of the CSM first, carving a path through which the bulk of the outer cocoon subsequently expands. As a result, the effective surface area of the outer cocoon's breakout could be reduced, which may diminish the strength of the associated shock breakout emission. This sequence of events may suppress its detectability.

texts.orig: 6.6. FBOT emission
texts.text: 6.6. FBOT emission

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Optical surveys have led to the discovery of rapidly evolving transients, typically characterized by durations of ≳ 10 days, luminosities of ∼ 10 42 -10 44 erg s -1 , and temperatures of ∼ 10 , 000-30 , 000 K. Their event rates have been estimated to be ∼ 4000-7000 Gpc -3 yr -1 (e.g., Drout et al. 2014; Pursiainen et al. 2018; Toshikage et al. 2024). More recently, a subclass of much faster transients ( ≲ 10 days), referred to as FBOTs, has been identified, with estimated event rates of ∼ 100 Gpc -3 yr -1 (Ho et al. 2023). In the following, we compare the properties of the jet-driven transients discussed here with these observational populations.

texts.orig: Comparing these features with observed transients suggests that the cocoon cooling emission discussed here is inconsistent with the rapidly evolving transients that have a timescale of ≳ 10 days (Drout et al. 2014; Pursiainen et al. 2018), due to the shorter duration (assuming M CSM ∼ 10 -2 -1 M ⊙ ), although the color and peak luminosity are broadly consistent. Reproducing the longer timescales would require either a much more massive CSM ( ≳ 1 M ⊙ ) or a significantly more extended progenitor. More importantly, jet-driven explosions are intrinsically rare ( ∼ a few × 100 Gpc -3 yr -1 ; Pian et al. 2006; Soderberg et al. 2006c; Cobb et al. 2006; Liang et al. 2007; Guetta & Della Valle 2007), and their event rates are not expected to exceed those of BL Type Ic SNe with which they are strongly associated ( ∼ 5000 Gpc -3 yr -1 ; Li et al. 2011; Shivvers et al. 2017; Perley et al. 2020; Corsi et al. 2023).

texts.orig: In conclusion, within the broader landscape of rapidly evolving blue optical transients, cocoon cooling emission from LGRB progenitors with extended CSM may represent the bright, blue, and fast end of the population, and could account for a significant fraction of the fastest FBOT events. This scenario has already been proposed in the literature, particularly for the early blue peak in GRB 060218 (Nakar 2015; see also Margutti et al. 2019, Suzuki & Maeda 2022, Suzuki et al. 2024). For more discussion in the context of EP240414a, see Hamidani et al. (2025).

texts.orig: Afterglow emission from the successful jet in (a), viewed either on or off-axis, or the mildly relativistic jet-cocoon in (b), could be very bright in optical bands and could also produce an FBOT-like emission (e.g., Sun et al. 2024; Srivastav et al. 2025; van Dalen et al. 2025; Bright et al. 2024; Zheng et al. 2025; Hamidani et al. 2025 for the case of EP240414a; and Busmann et al. 2025; Gianfagna et al. 2025; Xinwen et al. 2025; Yadav et al. 2025 for the case of EP241021a). In this case emission is non-thermal, from X-ray to radio bands. Therefore, radio/X-ray observations, together with optical observations, offer an opportunity to discriminate cooling emission (as in EP250108a; Srinivasaragavan et al. 2025) from afterglow emission; such observations could help pin down the nature of the jet-cocoon (failed, or successful) and infer its parameters. Hence, one could understand the reason for jet failure (e.g., in LLGRBs), as either due to intrinsically weak jet or due to thick/massive CSM (see Section 7.1).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7. DISCUSSION
texts.text: 7. DISCUSSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The observed event rates suggest that LLGRBs are ∼ 10 2 times more abundant than LGRBs (see: Coward 2005; Pian et al. 2006; Soderberg et al. 2006c; Cobb et al. 2006; Liang et al. 2007; Guetta & Della Valle 2007; without accounting for possible differences in the beaming factors). LLGRBs are thought to originate from failed jets, with one plausible scenario being that jet failure is caused by extended CSM (Nakar 2015). If jet properties are universal, this suggests that LGRB progenitors without extended/massive CSM might be rarer than those with CSM. However, this remains unclear due to the limited sample of stripped-envelope SNe with dense CSM, partly due to observational biases. One possibility is that LGRB-favored conditions (such as low metallicity; Savaglio et al. 2009; or high angular momentum MacFadyen & Woosley 1999), correlate with progenitors lacking extended CSM. Alternatively, LLGRBs might result from intrinsically weak jets, as discussed in Section 7.1.

texts.orig: Figure 8 shows the same results (as Figure 7) for a weak jet case ( E eng = 10 51 erg). Noticeable differences are: the transition from jet success to jet failure is shifted to lower radii (by a factor of ∼ 10 0 . 25 ∼ 1 . 7; see Equation (41)); the reduced values of Γ β in the failed/barely failed parameter space (because the ratio of energy to mass is lower here); and the lack of luminous FBOTs as a result of the much lower energy available for cocoon cooling emission (although less luminous FBOTs, similar to the first peak in GRB 060218 could still be produced, as well as afterglow powered non-thermal FBOTs; see Hamidani et al. 2025; Zheng et al. 2025).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the context of recent events detected by EP, we speculate that EP240414a might be consistent with a barely failed jet scenario, because of the intermediate intensity of its prompt emission and afterglow emission (relative to LLGRBs and LGRBs); and its association with an FBOT (Sun et al. 2024; Srivastav et al. 2025; van Dalen et al. 2025; Bright et al. 2024) whose early phase shows thermal emission (van Dalen et al. 2025). However, EP250108a (Eyles-Ferris et al. 2025; Rastinejad et al. 2025; Srinivasaragavan et al. 2025; Li et al. 2025), with its much softer prompt emission and thermal FBOT emission, appears rather consistent with conventional LLGRBs associated with failed jets. Considering an extended CSM, we discuss in details the parameter space for EP240414a in Hamidani et al. (2025), based on its observed FBOT, and the expected cooling and afterglow emissions from the jet-cocoon.

texts.orig: Starting from the jump conditions [equation (1)], we derived the jet head velocity in the CSM [equations (28) and (31)] and obtained a general formula applicable to both Newtonian ( β h ≪ 1) and relativistic ( β h -1 ≪ 1) regimes, as well as intermediate cases (Figure 2). We present a new refined expression for the breakout time [equations (33) and (34)], which, unlike previous works, includes the factor 1 -β h ; an important term that has not been considered in previous works focused on jets in massive stars, where β h ≪ 1. This advancement enables the first systematic analytic treatment of jet propagation across a wide range of CSM properties.
texts.text: Starting from the jump conditions [equation (1)], we derived the jet head velocity in the CSM [equations (28) and (31)] and obtained a general formula applicable to both Newtonian ( β h ≪ 1) and relativistic ( β h -1 ≪ 1) regimes, as well as intermediate cases (Figure 2). We present a new refined expression for the breakout time [equations (33) and (34)], which, unlike previous works, includes the factor 1 -β h ; an important term that has not been considered in previous works focused on jets in massive stars, where β h ≪ 1. This advancement enables the first systematic analytic treatment of jet propagation across a wide range of CSM properties.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our analysis shows that jet propagation in extended CSM exhibits fundamental differences from that in massive stars. Specifically, while jets in dense stellar interiors are characterized by slow head velocities ( β h ≪ 1), those in extended CSM can approach β h -1 ≪ 1 (see Figures 3). We find that the jet can still be successful even if t b ≫ t eng (left panel of Figure 5), against the classical success criterion ( t b < t eng for successful jets and t b > t eng for failed jets). To address this, we introduced a criterion based on the jet timescale t jet that reflects the health of the jet as it propagates in a given CSM [in terms of its physical size, equation (35) and its residual energy, equation (36)]. Consequently, the jet is determined as successful if t b < t eng / (1 -β h ); or failed otherwise (Figure 4). We alternatively expressed this criterion as a function of the CSM/jet parameters and found a strong dependence on R CSM for LGRB jets [equation (41)].

We find that the jet can still be successful even if t b ≫ t eng (left panel of Figure 5), against the classical success criterion ( t b < t eng for successful jets and t b > t eng for failed jets). To address this, we introduced a criterion based on the jet timescale t jet that reflects the health of the jet as it propagates in a given CSM [in terms of its physical size, equation (35) and its residual energy, equation (36)]. Consequently, the jet is determined as successful if t b < t eng / (1 -β h ); or failed otherwise (Figure 4). We alternatively expressed this criterion as a function of the CSM/jet parameters and found a strong dependence on R CSM for LGRB jets [equation (41)].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We examined the evolution of t jet and mapped the parameter space where jets either successfully break out or fail (right panel of Figure 5). We also evaluated the typical proper velocity Γ β of the jet-cocoon system [equation (42)]. Between the extremes of (a) successful jets (dominated by relativistic jet material; Γ β ∼ 10 -100) and (c) failed jets (dominated by the outer cocoon; Γ β ∼ 0 . 1), we identified (b) an intermediate case where the system is mildly relativistic (Γ β ∼ 1), driven by a combination of inner and outer cocoons (Figure 6). Following Eisenberg et al. (2022), we refer to this case as a 'barely failed' jet.

texts.orig: Our results place constraints on the CSM properties of cosmological LGRBs, assuming E eng ∼ 10 52 erg, and highlight the difference between their CSM and CSM observed in SNe Ibc (see Figure 7 and Section 1). However, because of possible variations in collapsar central engine energies, LLGRBs may arise either from conventional jets in dense CSM or from intrinsically weaker jets (see Section 7.1). Thus, the higher observed event rates of LLGRBs cannot be solely attributed to a higher abundance of collapsar systems with more CSM presence relative to collapsar systems with less CSM presence.
